schools.summary: Ellicott fixes the spring campaigning season, supplies the details of Samuel's fuller account, and argues the Chronicler's abrupt reintroduction of David presupposes the Samuel narrative.
Clarke notes that the Chronicler designedly omits the Bathsheba-Uriah episode Samuel places after this verse, stating only that David tarried at Jerusalem.
Henry draws the moral of the omission — we should not delight in repeating others' faults — and takes comfort that God corrects but will not abandon his people.

themes: the Chronicler's omission of David's sin
spring as the season of war
God's fidelity to his erring people

schools.summary: Ellicott handles the textual detail: the omitted Ishbi-benob episode, Gezer for Samuel's Gob, Sippai for Saph, the Rephaite descent, and the Chronicler's added 'they were subdued.'
Henry allegorizes the last giants as besetting sins mastered only by long struggle, insisting David's ordinary servants prevailed because God was on their side.

themes: gradual victory of the Son of David

schools.summary: Ellicott reads the crown as Milcom's/Moloch's, notes its talent weight is fit for an idol not a man, and proposes David either made a crown from it or had it held above him.
Wesley values the talent at 5,475 pounds; Clarke passes on the Targum's legend of a magnetic jewel that held the golden crown aloft, confessing he cannot explain it.

debates.question: Whose crown did David take — the Ammonite king's or the idol Milcom's?

sides.position: the crown of the human king of Ammon, weighed or valued as treasure
the crown of Milcom/Moloch, the Ammonite god, its talent weight fitting an idol's head

outliers.note: Relays without endorsement the Targum's claim that a magnetic precious stone held the woven gold of the crown aloft in the air.

schools.summary: Ellicott renders the verse as torture — sawing, harrowing, and axing the Ammonites — refuses to palliate the 'revolting savagery' but sets it as the age's 'glorious revenge,' and reports Samuel's one-letter variant 'set them among' the tools.
Clarke declines to expound the horror, referring the reader to his notes on the parallel in 2 Samuel 12.

debates.question: Did David torture the captured Ammonites with saws and axes, or set them to hard labour with such tools?

sides.position: judicial torture and execution — 'sawed with the saw'
forced labour — the one-letter variant 'set them in/among' the saws
holders: the reading of 2 Samuel 12:31 (as reported by Ellicott)

schools.summary: Ellicott canvasses three solutions to the Elhanan/Goliath crux: a fluctuating tradition preserved by Samuel, a corrupt Samuel to be corrected from Chronicles, or 'Elhanan' as an original name of David making Chronicles an unsuccessful emendation.
Clarke reports the Targum's tradition identifying Elhanan with David, who slew Lahmi the brother of Goliath on the same day as Goliath himself.

debates.question: Who did Elhanan slay, and how is 1 Chronicles 20:5 reconciled with 2 Samuel 21:19?

sides.position: Chronicles preserves the true reading — Elhanan slew Lahmi, Goliath's brother — and Samuel's text is corrupt
holders: scholars who correct Samuel from Chronicles (as reported by Ellicott)

sides.position: Samuel's text stands and reflects a popular tradition fluctuating between David and Elhanan as Goliath's slayer
'Elhanan' is the original name of David; Samuel first read 'son of Jesse... slew Goliath,' making the Chronicles reading an unsuccessful emendation

schools.summary: Ellicott notes Samuel's slightly fuller phrasing, that Chronicles abridges the count of the twenty-four digits, and that 'son of the giant' means born to the Rephaite clan.
Clarke refers the reader to his note on 2 Samuel 21:20 for the six fingers and six toes.
Henry's section-level reflection dismisses the extra digits as no advantage against Omnipotence.

themes: the futility of size against God

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the rare word 'these,' that Samuel reads 'these four,' revealing the Chronicler omitted one giant, and that 'the giant' means the Rephaite clan whose members need not have been brothers.
Clarke confirms the descent from 'Rapha' via the versions and defends the Chronicler's deliberate omission of Amnon's incest and Absalom's revolt as matters already recorded and unfit to repeat.
